item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Could have just terminated her or kept her outside until her termination date to see what happens if the subject never passed through a door

However at +400 it's unlikely to ever be changed regardless of how many comments ask for it. I still added a +1 which ruined the "404 not found" rating it had tho

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I never saw this reply to my comment (so pardon my extremely belated reply!), but:

That's true (the article does establish that!), but there's still no genuine reason for the Foundation to force her through the door. It's going to happen one way or another.

Besides, you can just cut the final entry and make the scene where she tries to prove her innocence (and fails, for eternity) be the final bit. That's still deeply disturbing, and avoids that feeling of titillation/exploitativeness.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is quite an interesting entry. The description always seemed a little short, but the logs really flesh it out. I did have an issue, though…

The last entry. While the "Find the one person who can prove your innocence" challenge is a bit ambiguous, it does create some doubt. Even without that, however, it's unclear why exactly the Foundation decided to force her through the door. They've already established that the subject is sent to some anomalous location and that they seemingly can't track them. From a reader's perspective, we don't learn anything new about the anomaly from the log besides "experimental tracking device 1A12 ceased broadcasting", which doesn't really matter since we don't actually know how 1A12 works and what sort of events (teleported hundreds of miles away to an area set up by the creators? Moved into a pocket dimension? Sent back to the time and place where the events described in the rooms take place? Simply being wiped from existence?) would cause it to stop functioning.

The Foundation doesn't make any other attempts to figure out how the game works. They don't investigate any of the final locations in the normal world, or try to figure out the meaning behind the startup animation. The note that the last user "almost made it out" implies that there is a way to survive the game, but we never get any details about this.

Still a good SCP, but it felt like it was missing something at the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i have only two things to say about this entry: "yes", and "more please". this is one of the most inspiring entries on the site. shocked it has a rating this good and no tales. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm really not sure what you mean here? Like… It's established higher up in the article that someone who loses this game cannot be saved. Once you play the game, you end up HAVING to go through the door, one way or another…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This has always been my issue. Which is particularly frustrating, because everything before that moment is pretty great, and the second to last entry —

Find the one person who can prove your innocence. (Note: Subject failed to complete task in time allotted.)

— is such a perfect ending.

The reason this ending feels so exploitative to me is because even if she is guilty, it's pointlessly cruel — to the point of being anti-scientific. The final entry exists only to titillate the reader, which means the Foundation is effectively doing this to her for our entertainment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A well-written skip, ruined by the exploitative bout of unnecessary narrative cruelty in the final entry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Necro but I like to think of this one as a very mathematical and defined one. Nothing special happens. When you break down a wall to get out and eventually open a door, effects continue as normal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For the purposes of these logs, mundane rooms have not been recorded. For a full record of all rooms and tasks, consult file:

Needs a file, even if it doesn't link anywhere.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why would we do that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting concept.
I upvote but i still need to say what has been said cause it need to be highlighted more.
Why the hell toss innocent D-class through door that put her into kill or be raped situation.
The first D class seemed to enjoy rushing into the school and taking revenge.
The game seem to return the subject always into a room they successfully passed.
Yes, she might not be that hysterical cause she knew where her uncle in that room was, she could do something about it. But knowing that her life was a living hell… I would not want to return to any point of it anyway.
That said… lack of what happen when person get behind is open to debate too, would like some life altering-time twisting anomaly… sending subject back in time into their body in the exact scenario happening in the room? I need more of this SCP!!!
OH and… Did anyone thought about letting a buddhist or someone who know himself inside out play the game?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There is always a Door.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's actually no mindscrewry to be had here, it's rather straightforward. The description tells us that the game has people journeying through an increasingly personal reflection of their lives. The test logs are merely this playing out: we're offered bite-sized glimpses into the lives of the people playing the game; each log builds a story around the individual. Not sure what's confusing about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: mindscrewery is ususally a good thing, but here it is too high with too little payoff. this seems to have little else and I'm unclear a bit on how any why for a lot of it. And the screwry gets very extreme especially at the end. I feel I'm missing something, but tentative -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How about some E class personel or D class being used on it that arn't bad people?

Seams like one of those punishing type things, so what about some one that don't deserve that kinda punishment?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow! Finally something good after days of trying to read some interesting article. What would happen if one of the subjects that has completed the game was left in the wild with no doors to be open?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Each "objective" was painful and sharp. I experienced second-hand mortification from reading about the personal demons of these subjects. Very well done!

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: I don't have any better offers, but I feel like both of those are kind of forced, and don't work witht he flow of the article….

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
I know Bright said this is a deliberate reference to AWCY, but I don't like the link to the hub to confirm it - this is one of the cases where I think more subtlety is good. This isn't a clear AWCY piece of work, but more of a subtle reference, and I think the link to the hub would make it too obtuse and in your face.

No real objections to the other one, but there's probably more obscure Bright works that could be linked to - the 001 proposal is quite famous and read a lot.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Edits vetoed by author. Timer and crosslink tag removed.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Wow, this is a great skip and I don't want to really muck it up with bulky or inorganic crosslinks. An inline link to AWCY? at the "taught me how to be cool" reference would be pretty simple. Additionally, the name Tamlin certainly appears in a lot of Bright's other works, one of the bigger mentions being in his Factory 001 proposal, so a link there from the name Tamlin could also be a pretty simple crosslink. As such, I am recommending the following edit:

Changing this:

The game always begins with a dedication screen, containing the message "To Joey, who taught me how to be cool, and (name of last person to play the game), who almost made it out." The content then shifts to an animated scene. The silhouette of a humanoid stands on the deck of what appears to be an oil tanker. The screen turns bright white, then returns to the previous scene. A yellow wall, larger than the ship, has been added to the scene. The wall's appearance causes a wave to wash over the ship, and the wave carries the humanoid overboard. The screen fills with bubbles, and the words 'The Book of Tamlin' and 'Start Game' appear overlaid on the bubbles.

To this:

The game always begins with a dedication screen, containing the message "To Joey, who taught me how to be cool, and (name of last person to play the game), who almost made it out." The content then shifts to an animated scene. The silhouette of a humanoid stands on the deck of what appears to be an oil tanker. The screen turns bright white, then returns to the previous scene. A yellow wall, larger than the ship, has been added to the scene. The wall's appearance causes a wave to wash over the ship, and the wave carries the humanoid overboard. The screen fills with bubbles, and the words 'The Book of Tamlin' and 'Start Game' appear overlaid on the bubbles.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The tasks are so damn dark and evocative… this is probably the third time I've read this article since coming across it. This might just be my favorite from Series 2.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Find your Uncle Billy, before he finds you."

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Didn't know this was based on a RP thing but I felt it was done well. Only gripe was the end where they just condemned her to be molested which didn't make sense and felt a bit too evil on the foundation's part. I mean they already know what happens it felt redundant.

Other than that +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is honestly one of the more fucked up thing I have read and I love it. 1+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Massively late reply, but in Dr Davidson's theory the game would be fucking with her because the person could have proved her innocence, but he can't because SHE killed him, and as such he is not innocent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Your post here:
http://www.scp-wiki.net/forum/t-537578/scp-1590#post-1574251
I think.

If he is a Korean War Vet, why is the Foundation sending a geriatric Agent? The man must be in eighties or nineties. Wouldn't he have long been retired?

Also, AFAIK, there aren't any jungles in Korea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Where does it say he's not a korean war veteran?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Trying to understand how it works, and where the people go when they vanish? You know, testing it?

Happy 200+

Just reward for a well executed extrapolation of a nasty little piece of art.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am a little confused as to how Gibson could have been awarded a Purple Heart on deployment to Korea and not be a Korean War veteran.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't understand:

- Is it implied that the person is "lost forever, essentially dead" at the end when transmission/tracking contact is lost? If so, why are these idiots still going through?

- Why?

- Why keep using it if it just kills people in an "ironic" way?

I just can't get over this aspect of it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey! Not all of us are Satanic. >:-|

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Here's a fun fact:

Tamlin, or Tam Lin, is a Scottish folk hero…–ish person.

He was once a mortal man, before being abducted by the fairies who put him through various sort of shit, normal fairy trickery, but once every seven years they give a tithe to Hell. And this Hallowe'en, the tithe is Tam Lin.

We are the Fae, we capture people, we give them to Hell, which is what the house of Tamlin is, and this time there is no fair lady who will help the D-class.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What good is forcing her to spend the rest of her life locked in a single room? By pushing her through, they might learn something… Probably did.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting, but the last log is so over-the-top "The Foundation is being evil for no reason" that I had to give it a -1. I mean really, what purpose is achieved by forcing a woman to get molested for all of eternity?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The last part made me really really sad!

Upvoted none the less

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I found this overhyped. wasn't good enough for an upvote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: IT started in the RP… this is the only way I found to cross it over, and make it good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually didn't know that Tamlin House was an SCP. I thought it was just an RP-type thing. Upvoted, because I like it a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You assume correctly!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i'm assuming 66,666 numbers being contained in a 1kb file is part of the anomaly besides the fact that the program shouldn't execute being just a series of digits

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1 Unique idea.

I liked everything but the senseless tossing of the final Class D into the horror room. The Foundation already figured out that they can't track people who enter it, why throw her in, too? I was waiting to see what happened if you just confined someone to the room they tested in and brought them food and water for eternity. You even hint that this might've been tried when you say that subjects don't _have_ to enter the horror room. It wouldn't be hard to say that they're confined to the test room until they either die or enter the door, or that after 100 hours they lose sanity and actively try and find a door to enter, and if you strap them down, they just turn into gibbering crazies. Or even have a test log where they try it, but the results are redacted. It just seems like an oversight on the foundation's part.

But overall it's a small nitpick, really.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hm. I wonder. What would happen if you -built- a room with no doors, and played the game there?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh. On re-reading, I remember there was a reason I had the Project Director changed… but I cannot rememeber what it was.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel the 'real' house is too… much to be translated well into an article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man, this is one way to write Tamlin House up as an SCP, innit? It's an angle I didn't expect, because game scips are kinda cliche and this could've just been done as the house, but the way this is executed makes up for that. I really love the log examples, and although some of them do feel kinda cliche it overall worked quite well for me… but that last room feels like a cop-out to not write something interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes Gene. It's anomalous. That's the point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe it's one of the anomalous properties. Inconsistent size when opened vs the size it seems to occupy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1590 is a one kilobyte program…
Attempts to view SCP-1590's coding reveals only the numbers 1 through 66,666 in numerical order.

Something's wrong here. It's got 9 one-digit numbers, 90 two-digit numbers, 900 three-digit numbers, 9000 four-digit numbers and 56 666 five-digit numbers. Which amounts to 322 kilobytes something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is seriously terrifying, especially "Find uncle Billy - before he finds you". +1.

item.title: And.... 100
item.content:encoded: Woot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mhmm. Yes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All horror is personal.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Uppity-upvoted because of those wonderful logs.

2 things:

- What everyone else has already said on the poor, innocent D-Class.

- What happens if the subject takes whatever he is carrying with him, could he pick up someone else and carry them through? Or vice versa, what happens if the person passes through while carried by someone else?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Doesn't necessarily have to have taken place during the Korean war.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvote. I don't like this as an AWCY? object, but the addenda make it work.

The thing about the agent serving in Korea confuses me. Either he's in his mid-eighties at this point, or this is from an article in the early- to mid-80's. It's probably the former, which clashes with my headcanon about the origins of AWCY? but whatever. And yes, I am thinking about this way to hard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: but even if that person is dead, that means that there was (at one point) someone who could prove her innocent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I read it as the game fucking with her because the person in question was dead.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one has that feeling of bleakness that I haven't felt since reading 231 for the first time. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But there are no other unsolvable puzzles listed: There's no mention of them ever being impossible. Why would the game suddenly break it's MO on this one case?
